← Back
NOTES
2026

Infrastructure as product

Platform work gets easier when it is treated like product work: clear users, measurable outcomes, and real ownership.

PlatformTerraformKubernetes

When infrastructure is treated like a product, it gains clarity: who it serves, what problem it solves, and how success is measured. Without that clarity, “platform” becomes a dumping ground for urgent work and invisible maintenance.

The first step is identifying customers. Internal customers are still customers: application engineers, data teams, security, support, and sometimes even finance. If you cannot name the user, it is hard to make good prioritization decisions.

Next is the product promise. A platform is usually trying to deliver one or more of these: faster delivery, safer change, lower operational toil, better compliance, or lower cost. Trying to deliver all of them at once often produces a platform that excels at none.

Once you have a promise, you can define outcomes. Outcomes are not “migrate to Kubernetes” or “adopt Terraform.” Outcomes look like “new services go to production in under a day,” “incident rate drops,” or “onboarding a new engineer takes hours, not weeks.”

Treat interfaces as first-class. Your platform’s API may be a CLI, a set of Terraform modules, a Kubernetes CRD, or a web portal. Whatever it is, it should be versioned, documented, and designed to be hard to misuse.

Good platform products offer a paved road. The paved road is the default path that is safe, supported, and fast. You can still leave the road, but the cost is explicit: additional ownership, extra review, or extra operational responsibility.

Developer experience is not aesthetics; it is throughput. Reduce the number of steps needed to create a service, get credentials, deploy, and observe. Every manual step is a point of failure and a tax on iteration.

Self-service is a capability, not a slogan. If a team must wait on another team for environments, secrets, or pipelines, you have built a queue. The product goal is not “control,” it is “safe autonomy.”

Reliability needs service-level thinking. If your platform has uptime requirements, define SLOs and error budgets. Treat planned maintenance, incident response, and change management as part of the product, not as side tasks.

Security is easier when it is built into defaults. Provide secure-by-default templates: hardened container base images, least-privilege IAM patterns, secret management that is integrated, and policy checks that run automatically.

Cost is part of the product too. Make cost visible and attributable: per environment, per team, per service. You do not need perfection; you need a feedback loop that helps teams see trade-offs and make informed decisions.

Roadmaps matter, but so does unplanned work. Platform roadmaps should include “keep the lights on” work explicitly: upgrades, deprecations, patching, capacity, and reliability improvements. If it is not on the roadmap, it still exists—it just becomes surprise work.

Adoption is the real KPI. A platform that teams avoid is not delivering value, no matter how elegant the architecture is. Measure adoption with real signals: how many services use the paved road, how often teams bypass it, and why.

Feedback loops should be continuous. Set up lightweight channels: office hours, short surveys after onboarding, and structured “top pain points” reviews every quarter. Platform teams that ship without feedback eventually ship the wrong things faster.

Ownership must be unambiguous. If the platform is “everyone’s responsibility,” it becomes nobody’s responsibility. Clear ownership includes support expectations, response times, and a definition of what the platform team owns versus what product teams own.

Finally, keep the scope honest. The best platform products are narrow at first: one great golden path, one great developer workflow, one reliable way to observe. Once that foundation is trusted, expansion becomes a natural consequence instead of a risky bet.

Infrastructure as product is not a rebranding exercise. It is a commitment to users, interfaces, outcomes, and iteration—the same disciplines that make external products successful.
